straight_beat.name: Harold 'Hal' Zorich
straight_beat.column:
The Reds lost yesterday, 10-2 to the Angels. That’s the Queen City ledger. Not that it matters much, but the numbers tell a story. The Angels’ offense didn’t just dominate; they carved up the Reds’ pitching like a Cincinnati hot dog at a summer festival. Ten runs? That’s not a game. That’s a statement. And the Reds? They looked like a team that’s been waiting for the phone to ring, hoping someone would call and say, “Hey, we need you.”  

The pitching staff, once a cornerstone of this team, now seems like a collection of guys who’ve forgotten how to pitch. The Angels’ bats didn’t just beat them—they embarrassed them. A few hits, a few walks, and suddenly you’re looking at a 10-2 score. The Reds’ starters didn’t hold serve, and their bullpen? Well, let’s just say the closer’s name isn’t in the headlines. This isn’t about one game. It’s about a trend. The Reds have been struggling to keep runs off the board, and it’s getting frustrating.  

But here’s the thing: The Reds are still in the race. 8-6, half a game back in the Central. That’s not a death sentence. It’s a warning. The front office has to figure out how to fix this. If they don’t, the fans will start asking questions. And not the nice kind. The team’s offense has been inconsistent, but there’s talent here. De La Cruz homered, Greene pitched into the sixth, and Cincinnati beat the Brewers 5-3 at Great American Ball Park. Those moments matter. They’re the reminders that this team can still compete.  

The problem is consistency. The Reds can’t keep

optimist.name: Candy Vanicek
optimist.column:
Elly De La Cruz ran from first to third in a blink, and I swear I could hear Rose cussing from the beyond. This is Cincinnati baseball reborn.  

Yeah, I know the Angels handed us a 10-2 whipping yesterday, but let’s not forget what we saw in that game. Elly didn’t just steal second base—he did it so smooth, you’d think he’d been doing it since he was a kid. And when he legged out a double to center, the way he kept his head up, like he was already planning the next hit, that’s the kind of grit that builds champions. The kid’s got the whole damn league fooled. You think the Angels’ pitchers were ready for him? They weren’t. And that’s a damn good sign.  

Now, I’ll be the first to admit, losing a game like that stings. But here’s the thing: we’re not just a team with a 8-6 record. We’re a team with a heartbeat. The way the offense is clicking lately? It’s like someone hit a switch. Those young guys in the middle of

pessimist.name: Earl Huffnagle
pessimist.column:
The Angels handed us another lesson in how to build a team that doesn’t just win but *is* a team. You know, the kind of team that plays with identity, not just payroll. We’re still trying to figure out what we’re supposed to be, and by the time we do, the season’s halfway over.  

Since 1975, we’ve been stuck in this endless loop of hope and disappointment. The ’75 team was magic, a flash in the pan that left us with a taste for something we’ve never been able to replicate. Every time we think we’ve found a formula—whether it’s developing young talent, trading for stars, or just staying competitive—we get blindsided by the same old mistakes. The front office acts like they’re running a chess game, but all they’ve ever done is shuffle pieces around a board that’s been set up to lose.  

Take the last few years.
